verses.text: Azariah reigns long in Judah and does right, though the high places remain and the people still sacrifice there. His reign is mostly faithful but tolerant of an old compromise. Half-cleansed worship leaves a door open that later generations will widen.

Azariah reigns long in Judah and does right, though the high places remain and the people still sacrifice there. His reign is mostly faithful but tolerant of an old compromise. Half-cleansed worship leaves a door open that later generations will widen.

The LORD strikes Azariah with leprosy, and he dwells apart while his son governs, until he dies. Even a good king bears a humbling affliction. Greatness does not exempt a man from God's correcting hand.

The LORD strikes Azariah with leprosy, and he dwells apart while his son governs, until he dies. Even a good king bears a humbling affliction. Greatness does not exempt a man from God's correcting hand.

Zechariah of Israel reigns six months and is murdered by Shallum — and so ends Jehu's house in the fourth generation, exactly as God had said. The promise and its limit both come true. God keeps His word in mercy and in judgment alike, to the very generation.

Shallum reigns a single month before Menahem kills him and seizes power with brutal cruelty. The northern throne becomes a bloody revolving door. A kingdom that forsook God tears itself apart, king devouring king.

Menahem's reign is evil, and when Pul of Assyria comes, he buys him off with a thousand talents of silver to secure his grip. He leans on the very empire that will one day destroy Israel. Fearful alliances only feed the power that will consume us.

Menahem's reign is evil, and when Pul of Assyria comes, he buys him off with a thousand talents of silver to secure his grip. He leans on the very empire that will one day destroy Israel. Fearful alliances only feed the power that will consume us.

Menahem exacts the silver from the wealthy of Israel, fifty shekels a man, then Assyria withdraws. The people are taxed to prop up a faithless king's throne. Sin at the top is paid for by the burdens it lays on those below.

Pekahiah reigns two years and is slain by his captain Pekah. Again a king falls to a conspirator. The relentless violence shows a society unravelling, with no fear of God to restrain the grasping of power.

Pekahiah reigns two years and is slain by his captain Pekah. Again a king falls to a conspirator. The relentless violence shows a society unravelling, with no fear of God to restrain the grasping of power.

Under Pekah, Tiglath-pileser of Assyria seizes much of the land and carries people captive; then Hoshea conspires and kills Pekah. The exile has begun in pieces. God's long-warned judgment closes in, tribe by tribe, as the nation crumbles.

In Judah, Jotham does right like his father, though the high places remain, and he builds the higher gate of the temple. A faithful king tends the house of God. Steady, quiet faithfulness, even amid old shortcomings, is honoured by the LORD.

In Judah, Jotham does right like his father, though the high places remain, and he builds the higher gate of the temple. A faithful king tends the house of God. Steady, quiet faithfulness, even amid old shortcomings, is honoured by the LORD.

Jotham's reign sees the first stirrings of Syria and Israel against Judah, and then he dies, succeeded by Ahaz. The storm clouds gather as a good reign ends. The next chapter shows how differently a faithless son meets the gathering danger.
